title: How India and Bangladesh frame the same story differently
hook: A single Bangladesh event, two very different national narratives — and where the framing gap creates information-security risk.

steps.title: The event
steps.body: A series of killings in Bangladesh (Awami-linked figure Osman Hadi; Hindu victims Dipu Das and Amrit Mondal) became a cross-border flashpoint: India voiced concern over 'continuous violence against minorities' and pursued Hadi's suspected killers on Indian soil, while Dhaka called Delhi's characterisation 'motivated' and referred the Hadi murder to the OHCHR.

steps.title: Bangladeshi framing vs Indian framing
steps.body: Bangladeshi outlets emphasise: Treating the deaths as domestic criminal/political-murder cases under investigation; The government seeking international (OHCHR) assistance into the Hadi killing. Indian outlets emphasise: A pattern of 'continuous violence against minorities' (Dipu Das, Amrit Mondal); India's official concern and cross-border pursuit of Hadi's killers (NIA custody, Bongaon arrests).

steps.title: The framing gap
steps.body: On the same set of killings, Indian outlets frame events as a pattern of anti-minority communal violence warranting Indian concern and cross-border action, while Bangladeshi outlets and government treat them as discrete crimes, call Delhi's characterisation 'motivated', and route the Hadi case to the OHCHR. This is arguably the highest-stakes information-security case: BD fact-checkers repeatedly documented that individual communal-attack claims (misidentified victims, old or scripted videos, AI content) were false or amplified in Indian media, so the 'framing gap' here is not just emphasis but a contested factual record about whether communal persecution is occurring at all.

steps.title: Grounded against fact-checks
steps.body: This narrative connects to 12 Rumor Scanner fact-checks of false/misleading claims — the debunk layer that separates verified fact from amplified framing.

title: A communal rumor, from spread to fact-check
hook: "Scripted, AI and miscontextualised videos alleging attacks on Hindus" — risk 86/100. How a sensitive claim spreads and is checked against known misinformation.

steps.title: The narrative
steps.body: Staged clips, AI content and unrelated footage from India, Nepal and elsewhere are passed off as evidence of mobs attacking Hindus and minorities in Bangladesh.

steps.title: Why it matters
steps.body: Fabricated visual 'proof' of anti-minority violence is highly persuasive, spreads fast, and feeds narratives used to pressure Bangladesh internationally.

steps.title: Explainable risk
steps.body: Risk 86/100 (high). Fabricated visual proof · Foreign-footage laundering · Communal incitement · Cross-border reach. Persuasive fabricated video evidence of anti-Hindu violence, laundered from foreign footage, maximises virality and communal harm.

steps.title: Matched to Rumor Scanner
steps.body: 12 related fact-checks provide verdicts (false / misleading) with evidence — the system never asserts a claim is false without the fact-check behind it.

title: An internal political narrative over time
hook: "Contested legitimacy of the February 2026 national election" — how a contested political narrative moves across outlets, figures, and borders.

steps.title: The narrative
steps.body: A recurring storyline disputing the fairness of the 13th parliamentary election through fabricated claims of rigging, postponement, recounts and forged foreign verdicts.

steps.title: Public figures connected
steps.body: Party pages and political figures amplify or contest this narrative — traced from their public posts in the Figure Tracker (evidence only, no judgement).

steps.title: Cross-border dimension
steps.body: Reporting the identical 12 February vote, Bangladeshi outlets frame it as a legitimate, internationally endorsed democratic transition (BNP mandate plus a 'Yes' referendum), while Indian outlets foreground vote-share math, Jamaat's rise and what a BNP win means for Delhi's strategic position. The gap matters because the legitimacy of a transitional election is itself contested information terrain; Indian framing seeds doubt about credibility and an Islamist-rise angle, and fabricated poll-related quotes and communal-attack claims were debunked by BD fact-checkers.

steps.title: Ask the copilot
steps.body: “How do Indian and Bangladeshi outlets frame the 2026 election differently?” — the Analyst Copilot answers with citations and a confidence level.
